summary: Jeff Atwood argues that ideas are vastly overvalued compared to execution, using the Fundable vs Kickstarter story as a cautionary tale. He dismisses the notion that internet fame or connections guarantee success, emphasizing that even well-known founders must execute well or fail. Drawing on insights from Derek Sivers and Pixar's Ed Catmull, Atwood contends that great teams can transform mediocre ideas into successes while mediocre teams will ruin even brilliant concepts. He credits Stack Overflow's success not to the idea or his fame, but to assembling the right team of developers he had worked with before.
key_points: Ideas without execution are nearly worthless - execution is the true multiplier of value
The Fundable founder's failure to recognize his own execution problems illustrates a common blind spot among entrepreneurs
Internet fame and connections don't grant immunity from the need to execute well
Software execution means obsessing over every tiny detail that makes up your application
Building great teams is the foundation of successful execution - they can fix mediocre ideas or discard them for better ones
money_quotes: Ideas are worth nothing unless executed. They are just a multiplier. Execution is worth millions.
If you give a good idea to a mediocre group, they'll screw it up. If you give a mediocre idea to a good group, they'll fix it.
I don't care how internet famous you are; nobody gets a pass on execution.
This is all your app is: a collection of tiny details.
In software development, execution is staying on top of all the tiny details that make up your app.
The disconnect between idea and execution here is so vast it's hard to understand why the author himself can't see it.
If you're not constantly obsessing over every aspect of your application, relentlessly polishing and improving every little part of it – no matter how trivial – you're not executing.
key_insight: Stop protecting your ideas and start building great teams, because a strong team can fix or replace a mediocre idea, while a weak team will destroy even a brilliant one.
